1: A type: after a long time — when YHWH had given Israel rest from all their surrounding enemies — Joshua summoned all Israel. The rest-gift that enables Joshua’s farewell is the Canaan-rest that Hebrews identifies as the provisional fulfillment pointing to the eschatological rest. Hebrews 4:8: “if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day later on.” The rest of Canaan (real, historical, comprehensive — YHWH gave it) is not the final rest but the pattern and pledge of it. The “long time afterward” before Joshua’s farewell is the fullness of the land-rest period; the “another day” of Psalm 95 and Hebrews 4 is the fullness of the Christ-rest that remains.
2: A type: Joshua summoned all Israel — its elders, leaders, judges, and officers. The full covenant leadership assembly for Joshua’s farewell mirrors the full assembly at Sinai and the Shechem renewal (Joshua 24). The death-discourse of the covenant leader — the farewell speech that becomes the covenant community’s inheritance — is the form of Moses’s Deuteronomy farewell and Christ’s upper-room discourse (John 13-17). The dying leader’s last words are not biography but covenant theology: what Israel needs to know to survive and thrive in the land is what Joshua teaches in his dying speech.
3: A type: “You yourselves have seen all that YHWH your God has done to all these nations for your sake, for it is YHWH your God who has fought for you.” Joshua’s summary of the conquest as YHWH fighting for Israel is the theological verdict that the military narrative embodies. Romans 8:31: “If God is for us, who can be against us?” is the NT form of this summary: the rhetorical question assumes the answer of Joshua 23:3 as its premise. Paul’s confidence in God’s advocacy is grounded in the conquest history — the pattern of YHWH fighting for his people has its ultimate expression in God’s not sparing his own Son (Romans 8:32).
4: A type: Joshua allotted to Israel the remaining nations as an inheritance for the tribes, from the Jordan to the Great Sea in the west. The inheritance of what is not yet possessed — the nations allotted as future inheritance even while some remain unconquered — is the form of the “already/not yet” eschatological inheritance. Hebrews 11:1: “faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.” The tribes receive their allotment as inheritance even before full possession — faith takes what the divine grant gives before experiential possession is complete.
5: A direct revelation (through Joshua): “YHWH your God will push them back before you and drive them out of your sight, and you shall possess their land, just as YHWH your God promised you.” The future-tense divine promise for the remaining conquest is the same form as Philippians 1:6: “he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ.” Joshua’s farewell does not minimize the remaining work but grounds the expectation of completion in the same YHWH who has already accomplished so much. The promise of completing what was begun is the structure of Christian hope.
6: A direct revelation: “Therefore be very strong to keep and to do all that is written in the Book of the Law of Moses, turning aside from it neither to the right hand nor to the left.” The strength-for-obedience formula (cf. Joshua 1:7: “be strong and very courageous, being careful to do according to all the law”) frames the covenant obligation as requiring active, sustained commitment to the written Torah. Matthew 5:17-18: “Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them... not an iota, not a dot, will pass from the Law until all is accomplished.” The total Torah-fidelity Joshua commands is answered in Christ’s perfect fulfillment — the completion that Joshua could command but not achieve.
7: A type: “that you may not mix with these nations remaining among you or make mention of the names of their gods or swear by them or serve them or bow down to them.” The four-fold prohibition against the remaining nations’ religious influence — mention, oath, service, worship — covers every level of religious engagement from speech to body. Second Corinthians Joshua 6:14: “do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers” — Paul’s application of the separation principle to the church extends Joshua’s covenantal warning. The warning against being shaped by the surrounding culture’s gods is perennial: every generation of the covenant community faces the same temptation to accommodate the idol-systems of its neighbors.
8: A type: “But you shall cling to YHWH your God just as you have done to this day.” The verb dabaq (cling/cleave) is the same word used in Genesis 2:24 (“a man shall leave his father and his mother and hold fast to his wife”) and Ruth 1:14 (Ruth “clung to” Naomi). The covenantal clinging is the marriage-fidelity metaphor: total attachment, irreversible loyalty. Romans 8:38-39: “neither death nor life... will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord” — the NT fulfillment is not Israel’s clinging to YHWH (which will fail, as the subsequent history shows) but Christ’s inseparable love clinging to his own. The dabaq becomes unconditional when Christ does the holding.
9: A type: YHWH has driven out before you great and strong nations, and no man has been able to stand against you to this day. The invincibility of Israel when YHWH fights for them is the historical testimony that grounds the covenant exhortation. The invincibility principle is not military but covenantal: Israel’s strength is entirely derivative of YHWH’s fighting-for. In Christ, the same pattern: “we are more than conquerors through him who loved us” (Romans 8:37) — the conquest is through the one who fights for us, not through our own military or spiritual capability.
10: A type: “One man of you puts to flight a thousand, since it is YHWH your God who fights for you, just as he promised.” The arithmetic of divine empowerment — 1,Joshua 000:1 — is the holy-war ratio that signals divine presence: normal military math is reversed when the divine warrior is the true combatant. Philippians 4:13: “I can do all things through him who strengthens me.” The one-against-a-thousand effectiveness is not the warrior’s skill but the divine presence’s empowerment. Paul’s “all things through him” is the NT form of the holy-war ratio: the believer’s disproportionate effectiveness comes entirely from the indwelling divine power.
11: A direct revelation: “Be very careful, therefore, to love YHWH your God.” The command to love YHWH — stated as the single most important exhortation in Joshua’s farewell — is the Shema’s heart distilled. First John 4:19: “we love because he first loved us” is the NT answer to Joshua’s command: the love that Joshua urgently commands is made possible by the prior love of Christ. The “be very careful to love” shows that love is not merely a feeling but a disciplined covenant commitment — and the ground of that disciplined love is the prior unconditional love of the one who commands it.
12: A type: if you turn back and cling to the remnant of these nations remaining among you and make marriages with them — the warning against covenant-mixing. The verb dabaq (“cling”) appears again but now negatively: the wrong clinging is to the nations instead of to YHWH. The ironic inversion is stark: the command of verse 8 is to cling to YHWH; the warning of verse 12 is against clinging to the nations. Second Corinthians Joshua 6:14: “do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers.” The clinging that belongs to YHWH being redirected to the nations is the fundamental covenant apostasy — and its practical form is intermarriage and social integration with those who serve other gods.
13: A type: the remaining nations will become “a snare and a trap for you, a whip on your sides and thorns in your eyes, until you perish from this good land.” The four metaphors of covenant curse from the remaining nations — snare, trap, whip, thorns — are the instruments of the judgment that the subsequent history will enact. First Corinthians Joshua 15:33: “do not be deceived: bad company corrupts good morals.” The same principle — proximity to false worship and practice corrupts covenant integrity — is Paul’s application to the Corinthian church. The thorns-in-eyes image echoes the wilderness experience (Numbers 33:55: “those you allow to remain will become barbs in your eyes and thorns in your sides”) — the same warning reiterated at the land’s reception.
14: A direct revelation (Joshua’s personal testimony): “I am about to go the way of all the earth, and you know in your hearts and souls, all of you, that not one word has failed of all the good things that YHWH your God promised concerning you; all have come to pass.” This is the OT’s most comprehensive single statement of divine promise-faithfulness: not one word failed. Second Corinthians Joshua 1:20: “For all the promises of God find their Yes in him [Christ].” Joshua’s territorial testimony (all land-promises fulfilled) is the type; Paul’s christological testimony (all covenant promises fulfilled in Christ) is the antitype. The lo nafal davar (not one word fell/failed) of Joshua’s dying testimony becomes the Yes and Amen of the gospel.
15: A type: just as all the good things that YHWH promised have come to pass for you, so YHWH will bring upon you all the evil things until he has destroyed you from off this good land. The double-edged faithfulness of YHWH — the same reliability that guarantees blessings guarantees curses — is Joshua’s most sobering teaching. Hebrews 10:28-29: “anyone who has set aside the law of Moses dies without mercy on the evidence of two or three witnesses. How much worse punishment, do you think, will be deserved by the one who has trampled underfoot the Son of God?” The principle of covenant symmetry — equal reliability of blessing and curse — is intensified in the new covenant: if YHWH’s land-curses were real and swift, the new covenant consequences for rejecting Christ are proportionally more severe.
16: A type: “if you transgress the covenant of YHWH your God, which he commanded you, and go and serve other gods and bow down to them. Then the anger of YHWH will be kindled against you, and you shall perish quickly from off the good land that he has given to you.” The final covenant warning is simple and direct: the path from idolatry to exile is short. Revelation 20:9: the final gathering of all who have aligned against God ends with fire from heaven consuming them — the eschatological form of the covenant curse Joshua warns about. The temporal land-loss Israel will experience from covenant violation is the historical enactment of a principle that has its ultimate expression in the final judgment, where those who serve other gods are excluded from the new creation.
